author: @rays6202
comment:
HERES THE FACTS HES LEAVING OUT 
CO2 makes up only about 0.04% of the atmosphere, and water vapor can vary from 0 to 4%. But while water vapor is the dominant greenhouse gas in our atmosphere, it has “windows” that allow some of the infrared energy to escape without being absorbed. In addition, water vapor is concentrated lower in the atmosphere, whereas CO2 mixes well all the way to about 50 kilometers up. The higher the greenhouse gas, the more effective it is at trapping heat from the Earth’s surface.

The burning of fossil fuels affects the concentration of CO2 in the atmosphere. Before the industrial revolution, the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere was about 288 ppm. We have now reached about 414 ppm, so we are on the way to doubling the amount of CO2 in the atmosphere by the end of this century. Scientists say that if CO2 doubles, it could raise the average global temperature of the Earth between two and five degrees Celsius. We are already increasing the amount of energy that bounces back to the Earth. Because of the greenhouse effect, this is causing global warming with its many destructive impacts.

What I find stunning and disturbing is the level of ignorance of the high number of people in this comment section who think that DL's "gotchya" question has any merit? If you believe him or his conclusion, you are being led down a factually wrong path.

Yes, the panel should've understood the science and they have fault in that, but his ignorance on the science is just as poor. They should all know why that tiny percentage, at first glance, seems like it's nothing, but it's an empirically demonstrable mistake to draw the conclusion that climate change isn't increasing due to increased human-induced carbon emissions. He should be speaking to climate scientists to understand the facts better and why his question is nonsense.

author: @JohnSmith-fj3uf

This is a cheap shot. I work in a chemistry lab unrelated to weather and report numbers in PPM. ( parts per million or milligrams per kilogram. ( then there are volume/ volume units too. ) 1 % is 10000 PPM. When you ask people  who use the numbers they often forget to think 1% is 10,000 0.1% is 1000. 0.04 % is 
400 PPM.  400 PPM CO2 is higher than it has been in many thousands of year. It is not surprising that under pressure someone who has been reading the in PPM has an awkward moment. On the left I am frustrated by people saying things like we need a state committee right away  to plan for sea level rise of I think it was a few Centimeters in the next 20 years.  On the right articles like this to make the problem seem small. 
In my life CO2 has got up from under 300 to over 400 PPM..Not a trivial amount. Exactly what the consequences will be I have not studied enough to form strong opinion other than it has got a measurable bit warmer.  Policy makers should avoid cheap shots like this to embarrass their opponents. It just clouds the discussion.

author: @gideonwilliams6307
comment:
So, we learn today that Doug LaMalfa does not understand that a fraction of a percent increase of CO2 can cause drastic changes in our ecosystem. 

Anyone whose studied meteorology knows that the reason we struggle so much to predict major weather events is bc they are specifically due to miniscule changes of pressure, temperature, etc. in our atmosphere that our best equipment even today cannot measure. That is simply the nature of a chaotic system (a system highly dependent on initial condition, think the butterfly effect). 

This is the kind of thing you get when you have representatives and just looking at the comments for this video, a populace, who has little to no experience learning about the science they are talking about and worse, think they're making good points.

This just reminds me of the insanity of that moment when a representative thought he was making a good point against global warming when he brought a snowball into congress.

author: @madsciencegary3830
comment: If plants didn't turn CO2 into oxygen and carbon (plants), we'd have a lot more carbon dioxide.  Since they do, we have an atmosphere with 20% oxygen and 0.04% carbon dioxide (nearly 80% nitrogen).  The slightly higher amount of CO2 results in better plant growth, and a more rapid conversion to oxygen.  Water vapor, on the other hand has concentrations 10-100 times higher than CO2, and actually absorbs more infrared energy than CO2 (which is how they claim it will cause global warming).  We don't worry about water vapor because they can't convince you it is our fault or get you to pay to reduce it.

author: @hobnob_
comment:
This is a frustrating video for lots of reasons.

The scientists were asked a pop quiz question they weren't prepared for, but they should still have known the answer to a question like that.

But what's most annoying is that the politician thinks he's got them, and people are cheering him on as if scientists are dumb. He dismisses the calls for reforms on account of a 0.01% increase of CO2 in the atmosphere, while also saying that a decrease of 0.01% sees plant life start to die off. Why does he accept that a 0.01% change in one direction is dramatic and yet dismiss the idea that the same change in a different direction is insignificant?

The framing of CO2 as a 0.04% problem is disingenuous. The issue isn't that it's small as a percentage of the atmosphere, the issue is the rate of change and the direction of that change. Using his same logic you could create a global scare that we're all going to get ammonia poisoning or the bends as a result of air being 70% Nitrogen.

He makes good observations about logistics, but he doesn't ask, 'How do we solve them?' He just disparages the scientists. The job of a politician is to look at technological change and make it workable, not dismiss scientific results because they make his job difficult.

People are holding him up as someone who has 'debunked' climate change as 'a hoax' but he's done nothing of the sort. He's gotten a few scientists to embarrass themselves and then used it to say, 'I'm not going to do what the data says,' data which isn't based on the atmosphere being 5% CO2 despite the scientists failing his arbitrary trivia night question.

Ecosystems and climates are finely balanced systems. Anyone who goes hunting and understands culling should understand that. If the balance fails, there are consequences that don't work well for humans. If you don't cull bears in regions where they are prevalent, there are more than can come into town, and it's bad for humans. If you don't cull wolves, they eat the deer and rabbits meaning there's less to eat, and it's bad for humans. If you allow the atmosphere to change and raise the global average temperature by a few degrees, plants dry out, ice caps melt, sea levels rise, hurricanes become more powerful as a result of warmer seas, forest fires increase, weather systems change (making it harder for people to harness the seasons to maximise their yields, like the farming politician in the video), and it's bad for humans.

Planet Earth will spin on. The issues are things like crop yields, ocean acidity and it's impact on the water cycle, and fish stocks to name only three. The issue is humanity's ability to thrive in a world where our centuries of knowledge of the natural world becomes less relevant and more obsolete.

author: @Dale-v6o
comment:
All living plants need Co2 to live on, which in turn returns oxygen for all animal life forms, including humans, to breathe. I've heard some kind of malarkey from Pesudoscientists saying the oceans are the main contributing factor for the oxygen in our atmosphere. Most marine life needs oxygen to live, and the wave action mixes atmospheric gases, including oxygen, keeping the waters oxygenated for marine life. A stagnant pool of water that's never moved wind or mechanical means will be very low on oxygen content.
